solution:
A-6 We understand the statement to mean that
\[
3=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots \sqrt{1+(n-1) \sqrt{1+n}}}} .}
\]

We see that
\[
\begin{aligned}
3 & =\sqrt{1+2 \cdot 4}=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{16=}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{25}}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+4 \sqrt{36 .}}}
\end{aligned}
\]

This leads us to conjecture the relation
\[
3=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots+\sqrt{1+n \sqrt{(n+2)^{2}}}}}} \quad \text { for all } n \geqq 1 .
\]

Proceedirg by induction we verify that \( (n+2)^{2}=n^{2}+4 n+4=1+(n+1)(n+3) \) \( =1+(n+1) \sqrt{(n+3)^{2}} \). This given, we must have
\[
3 \geqq \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{\cdots \sqrt{1+(n-1) \sqrt{(1+n)}}}}}
\]

To set an inequality in the other direction; observe that for any \( \alpha>1 \)
\[
\sqrt{1+n \alpha} \leqq \sqrt{\alpha} \sqrt{1+n}
\]

A repetition of this inequality gives then

descriptive_long.solution:
fixedconst-6 We understand the statement to mean that
\[
3=\lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots \sqrt{1+(indexvar-1) \sqrt{1+indexvar}}}} .}
\]

We see that
\[
\begin{aligned}
3 & =\sqrt{1+2 \cdot 4}=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{16=}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{25}}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+4 \sqrt{36 .}}}
\end{aligned}
\]

This leads us to conjecture the relation
\[
3=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots+\sqrt{1+indexvar \sqrt{(indexvar+2)^{2}}}}}} \quad \text { for all } indexvar \geqq 1 .
\]

Proceedirg by induction we verify that \( (indexvar+2)^{2}=indexvar^{2}+4 indexvar+4=1+(indexvar+1)(indexvar+3) \) \( =1+(indexvar+1) \sqrt{(indexvar+3)^{2}} \). This given, we must have
\[
3 \geqq \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{\cdots \sqrt{1+(indexvar-1) \sqrt{(1+indexvar)}}}}}
\]

To set an inequality in the other direction; observe that for any \( paramalpha>1 \)
\[
\sqrt{1+indexvar paramalpha} \leqq \sqrt{paramalpha} \sqrt{1+indexvar}
\]

A repetition of this inequality gives then

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
ballooning-6 We understand the statement to mean that
\[
3=\lim _{landscape \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots \sqrt{1+(landscape-1) \sqrt{1+landscape}}}} .}
\]

We see that
\[
\begin{aligned}
3 & =\sqrt{1+2 \cdot 4}=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{16=}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{25}}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+4 \sqrt{36 .}}}
\end{aligned}
\]

This leads us to conjecture the relation
\[
3=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots+\sqrt{1+landscape \sqrt{(landscape+2)^{2}}}}}} \quad \text { for all } landscape \geqq 1 .
\]

Proceedirg by induction we verify that \( (landscape+2)^{2}=landscape^{2}+4 landscape+4=1+(landscape+1)(landscape+3) \) \( =1+(landscape+1) \sqrt{(landscape+3)^{2}} \). This given, we must have
\[
3 \geqq \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{\cdots \sqrt{1+(landscape-1) \sqrt{(1+landscape)}}}}}
\]

To set an inequality in the other direction; observe that for any \( hummingbird>1 \)
\[
\sqrt{1+landscape\, hummingbird} \leqq \sqrt{hummingbird} \sqrt{1+landscape}
\]

A repetition of this inequality gives then

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
unknownvariable-6 We understand the statement to mean that
\[
3=\lim _{constantvalue \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots \sqrt{1+(constantvalue-1) \sqrt{1+constantvalue}}}} .}
\]

We see that
\[
\begin{aligned}
3 & =\sqrt{1+2 \cdot 4}=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{16=}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{25}}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+4 \sqrt{36 .}}}
\end{aligned}
\]

This leads us to conjecture the relation
\[
3=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots+\sqrt{1+constantvalue \sqrt{(constantvalue+2)^{2}}}}}} \quad \text { for all } constantvalue \geqq 1 .
\]

Proceedirg by induction we verify that \( (constantvalue+2)^{2}=constantvalue^{2}+4 constantvalue+4=1+(constantvalue+1)(constantvalue+3) \) \( =1+(constantvalue+1) \sqrt{(constantvalue+3)^{2}} \). This given, we must have
\[
3 \geqq \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{\cdots \sqrt{1+(constantvalue-1) \sqrt{(1+constantvalue)}}}}}
\]

To set an inequality in the other direction; observe that for any \( lastletter>1 \)
\[
\sqrt{1+constantvalue lastletter} \leqq \sqrt{lastletter} \sqrt{1+constantvalue}
\]

A repetition of this inequality gives then

garbled_string.solution:
A-6 We understand the statement to mean that
\[
3=\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots \sqrt{1+(qzxwvtnp-1) \sqrt{1+qzxwvtnp}}}} .}
\]

We see that
\[
\begin{aligned}
3 & =\sqrt{1+2 \cdot 4}=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{16=}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{25}}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+4 \sqrt{36 .}}}
\end{aligned}
\]

This leads us to conjecture the relation
\[
3=\sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{1+\cdots+\sqrt{1+qzxwvtnp \sqrt{(qzxwvtnp+2)^{2}}}}}} \quad \text { for all } qzxwvtnp \geqq 1 .
\]

Proceedirg by induction we verify that \( (qzxwvtnp+2)^{2}=qzxwvtnp^{2}+4 qzxwvtnp+4=1+(qzxwvtnp+1)(qzxwvtnp+3) \) \( =1+(qzxwvtnp+1) \sqrt{(qzxwvtnp+3)^{2}} \). This given, we must have
\[
3 \geqq \sqrt{1+2 \sqrt{1+3 \sqrt{\cdots \sqrt{1+(qzxwvtnp-1) \sqrt{(1+qzxwvtnp)}}}}}
\]

To set an inequality in the other direction; observe that for any \( rjtmcfok>1 \)
\[
\sqrt{1+qzxwvtnp rjtmcfok} \leqq \sqrt{rjtmcfok} \sqrt{1+qzxwvtnp}
\]

A repetition of this inequality gives then

kernel_variant.question:
Putnam-type problem.  

Show that the infinite nested radical whose successive outside coefficients are 6,7,8,\dots converges and that its value is 7; explicitly

                     7 = \sqrt{1+6\sqrt{1+7\sqrt{1+8\sqrt{1+9\sqrt{1+10\sqrt{1+\,\cdots}}}}}}\,.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let  
            F_k(x):=\sqrt{1+kx}\qquad(k\ge 6,\;x\ge 1),  
so that the desired radical is obtained by composing the maps F_6,F_7,F_8,\dots.

1.  Finite truncations and monotonicity.
   For n\ge 0 define
        R_n:=F_6\bigl(F_7(\dots F_{6+n}(7+n)\dots)\bigr),
so that
        R_0=\sqrt{1+6\cdot7},\; R_1=\sqrt{1+6\sqrt{1+7\cdot8}},\; \text{etc.}
Because every F_k is strictly increasing, replacing the innermost 7+n with the larger quantity
        F_{6+n+1}(8+n)>7+n
shows
        R_0<R_1<\dots<R_n<\dots\,.                       (1)
Hence the increasing sequence (R_n) has a finite or infinite limit
        R:=\lim_{n\to\infty}R_n.                           (2)

2.  A universal upper bound 7.
   Put
        S_n:=F_6\bigl(F_7(\dots F_{6+n}(8+n)\dots)\bigr)\qquad(n\ge 0).
Using the identity
        (k+1)^2=1+k(k+2)                                   (3)
one checks successively from the inside out that
        F_{6+n}(8+n)=6+n+1,
        F_{6+n-1}(6+n+1)=6+n,\;\dots,\;S_n=7.
Because 8+n>7+n, (1) implies
        R_n<S_n=7\quad(\text{all }n),                     (4)
so that R\le 7.

3.  A derivative bound.
   For k\ge 6 and x\ge k+1 we have
        F_k'(x)=\dfrac{k}{2\sqrt{1+kx}}\le\frac{k}{2\sqrt{1+k(k+1)}}=:d_k.   (5)
Since k^2<1+k(k+1) for every k\ge 1,
        0<d_k<\tfrac12.                                    (6)

   To apply (5) we must verify that every argument fed into F_{6+m} is at least 6+m+1.  Define
        A_m:=F_{7+m}(F_{8+m}(\dots)),                       (7)
i.e. A_m is the INPUT of F_{6+m}.  We claim
        A_m\ge 7+m= (6+m)+1 \qquad (m\ge 0).                (8)
   Proof by reverse induction on m.
        * Base step (m=n, the innermost stage): A_n=7+n by definition, so (8) holds.
        * Induction step.  Assume (8) for m+1.  Then
              A_m=F_{7+m}(A_{m+1}) \ge F_{7+m}(7+m+1).
           But
              F_{7+m}(7+m+1)=\sqrt{1+(7+m)(7+m+1)} > 7+m,   (9)
           so (8) is true for m as well.
Thus every input to F_{6+m} satisfies the inequality required for (5).

4.  Exponential decay of |7-R_n|.
   The radicals R_n and S_n differ only at the innermost entry, which is 7+n in R_n and 8+n in S_n; their difference is therefore
        0<7-R_n=|S_n-R_n|\le d_6d_7\dots d_{6+n}\cdot1.     (10)
Because each d_k<\frac12, the right-hand side is bounded by
        (\tfrac12)^{n+1}.                                   (11)
Hence |7-R_n|\to 0 as n\to\infty.  Combining (2), (4) and (11) yields
        R=\lim_{n\to\infty}R_n=7.                          (12)

Consequently the infinite nested radical converges and equals 7:
        \boxed{\;7=\sqrt{1+6\sqrt{1+7\sqrt{1+8\sqrt{1+9\sqrt{1+\,\cdots}}}}}\;.

5.  (Optional) Generalisation.
   Replacing the initial coefficient 6 by any integer a\ge 2 and repeating the argument gives
        a+1=\sqrt{1+a\sqrt{1+(a+1)\sqrt{1+(a+2)\sqrt{\,\cdots}}}}\,.

core_steps: View the infinite radical as the limit of its finite truncations.
Replace the last radical in a truncation by the perfect square (n+2)^2 so that its square-root collapses.
Verify inductively that √[1 + k(k+2)] = k+1, yielding every such ‘perfect-square truncation’ equal to the target constant.
Compare an ordinary truncation with its corresponding perfect-square version via the inequality √(1+nα) ≤ √α √(1+n) (α>1) to obtain upper and lower bounds.
Let n→∞; the bounds coincide, so the original infinite radical equals the constant.

slot1.description: Initial multiplier in the outermost radical (the sequence of multipliers then increases by 1 at each deeper level). All perfect-square substitutions and final value shift accordingly.

slot2.description: Limit of the infinite radical; always one more than slot1.
